serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nanjing Salted Duck Soup is approximately 400 ml (400 g), typically served as a main dish at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Nanjing Salted Duck Soup exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked duck (meat and skin) diluted to reflect a soup preparation (approximately 30–35% duck solids in broth). Sodium is elevated to reflect the salt-brining process characteristic of Nanjing salted duck. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against this USDA anchor and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from USDA duck fat profile. Cholesterol estimated from USDA cooked duck data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-based soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nanjing Salted Duck Soup. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate meat-based soups and broths. Carbohydrate content is 0.8 g per 100 g; glycemic load per 400 g serving = (15 × 3.2) / 100 = 0.48, rounded to 1.0. This is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classifications for protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate soups.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (480 mg per 100 g; ~1920 mg per 400 ml serving) due to the salt-brining process used for Nanjing salted duck
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this soup, particularly the broth, as a single 400 ml serving provides approximately 1920 mg sodium — exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; consuming only the duck meat and discarding most of the broth is advised if the dish is consumed at all.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (0.8 g per 100 g) and high protein (6.5 g per 100 g) with a low estimated glycemic index of 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: Nanjing Salted Duck Soup is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load and low glycemic index; however, the high sodium content warrants caution, and portion size should be moderated to manage overall dietary sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (480 mg/100 g) and moderate fat content (4.2 g/100 g including saturated fat ~1.2 g/100 g) from duck skin and meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content poses a significant risk for blood pressure elevation; consumption should be infrequent, portion-limited, and ideally restricted to the lean duck meat rather than the full broth to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium intake may be pro-inflammatory; ginger in the soup has documented anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Duck provides good-quality protein and minerals beneficial for joint health, and ginger may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit; however, the high sodium content may exacerbate inflammation in some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, so broth intake should be limited.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nanjing Salted Duck Soup (南京盐水鸭汤, Nánjīng yánshuǐyā tāng) is a traditional Chinese soup originating from Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, Eastern China. It is prepared by simmering pieces or the carcass of Nanjing Salted Duck — a celebrated regional specialty known for its tender, pale white meat and savory-sweet skin — in water with aromatics including ginger, scallions, star anise, and Sichuan peppercorns, and optionally white radish (daikon) or goji berries. The salt-brining process used to prepare the duck infuses the resulting broth with a distinctive rich umami flavour and a subtle floral aroma. The soup is characteristically clear yet intensely savoury, with a warming, nourishing quality prized in Nanjing culinary culture. Per 100 g, the soup provides approximately 65 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and only 0.8 g carbohydrate, making it a low-calorie, high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium content is notably high at 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1920 mg per standard 400 ml serving) due to the brining process, making it unsuitable for individuals on low-sodium diets or those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible. It is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management but should be consumed with caution by those with hypertension or cardiovascular conditions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Nanjing Salted Duck Soup exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked duck (meat and skin) data, adjusted for soup dilution and elevated sodium from the brining process. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against this USDA anchor and are broadly consistent. Confidence is rated 'medium' due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific soup preparation is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-16; the soup is predominantly water by volume but exact per-100g water content for this preparation is not available from an approved source. 2. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6 g/100g), trans_fat_g (0.0 g/100g), and cholesterol_mg (28 mg/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy duck (meat and skin, roasted) fatty acid profile, adjusted for soup dilution; these are not directly reported for this dish in any approved source. 3. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical '1 serving (400ml)' entries) has been deduplicated to a single 400 g serving. 4. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Nanjing Salted Duck Soup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. Dietary tag 'low_fat' from the input is retained with caution; at 4.2 g fat per 100 g the dish is moderate-to-low in fat relative to other duck preparations but not strictly 'low fat' by clinical standards. 6. Halal status is marked false as the brining and preparation process for traditional Nanjing salted duck is not certified halal; consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
